ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — custard tart / egg tart
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Portuguese egg tart specifically; A0FKP (pastry products, filled) used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Portuguese egg tart; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: pastry shell formed by laminating butter into dough
custard filling set by oven heat
main_ingredients: puff pastry (wheat flour, butter or margarine)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Portuguese egg tart weighs approximately 80 g and represents a typical single-serve portion as sold in bakeries across Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Portuguese Egg Tart — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Portuguese egg tart (葡式蛋挞) as a named entry with per-100g values matching the input record exactly. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 48% of total fat (10.5 g/100g) based on HPB typical puff-pastry composition guidance. Sub-fraction fatty acids (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) and all micronutrients beyond calcium and iron are not published by HPB Foodo for this entry; these fields are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Portuguese egg tart found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: custard tart / egg custard pastry (puff pastry base with egg-cream filling), consistent with GI values reported for similar short-crust and puff-pastry custard products (GI range 50–60) in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 56 × (30.6 × 0.80) / 100 = 56 × 24.48 / 100 ≈ 13.7 (serving size 80 g, available carbohydrate 24.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (13.5 g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 93 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low (93 mg/100g) and does not independently raise hypertension risk, but the high sugar load (13.5 g/100g) with minimal fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain per AHA 2018 guidelines; limit to one tart per occasion and avoid pairing with sweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (30.6 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g) and medium GI (~56) produces a significant postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of 30.6 g/100g refined carbohydrate, 13.5 g/100g sugar, and only 0.5 g/100g dietary fibre results in a medium-to-high glycemic load per serving (~13.7 GL units); individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit consumption to half a tart on an occasional basis and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (21.9 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100g and potential trans fat from puff pastry margarine
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 10.5 g/100g substantially exceeds the WHO recommended limit of 10% of total energy from saturated fat; combined with high sugar and refined carbohydrate, regular consumption of Portuguese egg tarts is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and increased cardiovascular risk — restrict to occasional treats only.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Portuguese egg tart (葡式蛋挞; pastel de nata) is a baked pastry originating in Lisbon, Portugal, and widely popularised across Macau, Hong Kong, Malaysia, and Singapore through Portuguese colonial influence and Cantonese bakery culture. It consists of a crispy, flaky puff-pastry shell filled with a smooth, lightly caramelised egg custard made from egg yolks, sugar, milk or cream, and vanilla. The pastry shell is formed by laminating butter or margarine into dough, producing distinct flaky layers, while the custard filling is set by high-heat baking that creates characteristic brown caramelised spots on the surface. Per 100 g, the tart provides 346 kcal, 6.6 g protein, 21.9 g fat (estimated 10.5 g saturated), 30.6 g carbohydrate, 13.5 g sugar, and only 0.5 g dietary fibre, making it a calorie-dense, high-fat, high-sugar pastry. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 56 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of about 13.7 per standard 80 g tart. Clinically, the high saturated fat content raises LDL cholesterol risk, the high sugar with negligible fibre creates rapid postprandial glucose elevation relevant to type 2 diabetes management, and the combination of saturated fat and refined carbohydrate is a concern for cardiovascular disease. Sodium is relatively low at 93 mg/100g. The tart is commonly consumed as a snack or dessert in Singaporean and Malaysian bakeries, dim sum restaurants, and café settings.

How much fat is in a Portuguese egg tart?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which lists Portuguese egg tart as a named entry with exact per-100g values matching the input record. Saturated fat estimated from HPB puff-pastry composition guidance. GI estimated from the closest analogous custard-tart entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Foodo does not publish monounsaturated fat sub-fractions for this entry, and no directly matching Portuguese egg tart entry with monounsaturated fat data was found in USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: HPB Foodo does not publish polyunsaturated fat sub-fractions for this entry, and no directly matching Portuguese egg tart entry with polyunsaturated fat data was found in USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. trans_fat_g set to -1: HPB Foodo does not publish trans fat values for this entry; trans fat presence is qualitatively noted in health context based on puff pastry margarine composition but a quantified per-100g figure is not available from any approved source as of April 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry and not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly matching product as of April 2026. cholesterol_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no directly matching approved-source entry with cholesterol data identified as of April 2026. vitamin_c_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; negligible in baked egg-and-pastry products but no confirmed zero from an approved source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_b6_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_b12_mcg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. phosphorus_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. magnesium_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. zinc_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source figure available as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; no approved-source moisture figure available as of April 2026. All -1 fields reflect genuine data gaps in the HPB Foodo database for this specific food entry; USDA FoodData Central was consulted as a secondary source but no directly matching Portuguese egg tart product entry with these sub-fraction values was identified. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100g) is estimated at approximately 48% of total fat based on HPB typical puff-pastry butter/margarine composition guidance; this estimate should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on custard tart / puff-pastry egg custard entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); a direct GI measurement for Portuguese egg tart has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (6.6), fat_g (21.9), and carbohydrate_g (30.6) are all non-zero.
